executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment totaled 75.7 million beneficiaries as of December 2025, with most major states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. California leads with 12.6 million enrollees but posted the steepest decline at -5.6% year-over-year, while Illinois also saw significant reduction at -6.5%. Texas maintained relative stability with 4.1 million enrollees and a modest -2.2% decline, outperforming larger states like California and Florida (-4.0%). Notably, North Carolina bucked the national trend with positive growth of +1.6%. National program expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a per-enrollee average of $2,851. Texas demonstrates cost efficiency at $2,950 per enrollee, positioning favorably against peer states like Pennsylvania ($4,368) and Ohio ($3,522), though trailing lower-cost states like Florida ($2,029) and North Carolina ($2,613).

Quality performance reveals significant interstate variation, with national scores averaging 65.6 out of 100 points. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a score of 62, placing in the moderate performance tier but trailing regional peers and falling short of high-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80). The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong infrastructure for care coordination that may not yet be translating to optimal quality outcomes. Current regulatory activity indicates continued federal focus on interoperability standards and prior authorization processes, while procurement signals suggest active vendor engagement across multiple states, positioning for potential program enhancements in the coming fiscal period.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.08 million enrollees, representing a significant decline from pandemic-era peaks with a -2.2% year-over-year decrease that aligns closely with the national unwinding trend. The state's per-enrollee expenditure of $2,950 sits above the national average of $2,851 but remains competitive compared to peer states—notably outperforming expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,368) and Ohio ($3,522) while spending more than fellow non-expansion states Florida ($2,029) and North Carolina ($2,613). Texas demonstrates operational efficiency through its robust managed care infrastructure, with 93.3% penetration rates significantly exceeding the national average of 83.2%, indicating strong administrative capabilities in care coordination and cost management.

However, quality outcomes present Texas's most significant challenge, with a quality score of 62/100 ranking 30th nationally and falling below the national average of 65.6. This positions Texas in the bottom tier among large states, trailing California, New York, and even non-expansion peer Florida in care quality metrics. The state's performance mirrors broader regional patterns, as southern non-expansion states dominate the bottom quintile of quality rankings. Key areas to monitor include whether Texas can leverage its strong managed care penetration to drive quality improvements, how federal regulatory changes around interoperability and prior authorization will impact the state's cost management strategies, and whether ongoing procurement activities signal investments in program modernization that could address the quality-cost balance that currently defines Texas's Medicaid landscape.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,368 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,851, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: Florida Extremely Low Per-Enrollee Spending
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,029 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating inadequate coverage or access issues.

alerts.title: Five States Have Critically Low Quality Scores
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, well below the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Illinois Enrollment Declined 6.5% Year-Over-Year
alerts.description: Illinois experienced a significant enrollment decline of 6.5%, indicating potential eligibility or access barriers affecting coverage.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Combines High Quality with High Costs
alerts.description: Massachusetts leads in quality scores (81) but has the second-highest per-enrollee spending at $4,185, suggesting a potential quality-cost tradeoff model.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score lags at 62/100, ranking #30 nationally
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid quality performance is below the national average of 65.6, ranking 30th out of 50 states. This places Texas in the 'moderate' tier, well below high-performing states like MA (81) and MN (80).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality improvement initiatives will be critical for contract renewal and regulatory compliance. HHSC likely faces pressure to improve outcomes given the size of the program.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement strategy to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining at -2.2% YoY with 4.08M current enrollees
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 2.2% year-over-year to 4,083,939 enrollees. This decline aligns with national unwinding trends but impacts program scale and revenue projections.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment decline affects contract value and service delivery planning. Accenture needs to adjust resource allocation and help HHSC manage the transition effectively.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review workforce planning assumptions with HHSC operations team

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs at $2,950 exceed national average by $99
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,950 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,851. Total expenditures reach $12.05B annually across 4.08M enrollees.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Above-average per-enrollee costs suggest opportunities for cost management initiatives. This data supports business cases for efficiency improvements and value-based care programs.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost management opportunities with HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: New federal interoperability and prior auth rules published April 2026
executiveInsights.summary: CMS has published new federal regulations on interoperability standards and prior authorization requirements affecting Medicare and Medicaid programs. Implementation timeline and compliance requirements need immediate review.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New regulatory requirements will impact system configurations and operational processes. Early preparation is essential to ensure Texas remains compliant and avoids penalties.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag regulatory compliance review to HHSC IT and policy teams

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration at 93.3% exceeds national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration (3.86M enrollees) compared to the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong managed care adoption but requires robust oversight capabilities.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration creates opportunities for value-based initiatives but demands sophisticated plan management and monitoring capabilities to ensure quality and compliance.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care oversight processes with HHSC plan management team

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% MC penetration creates major contract renewal opportunities as existing MCO contracts approach expiration cycles.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Proactively engage with HHSC procurement teams to position for upcoming managed care RFPs.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating regulatory pressure and improvement mandates.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting specific CMS measures where Texas underperforms.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline stabilization
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's -2.2% YoY enrollment decline requires operational efficiency improvements to maintain program viability.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Implement advanced analytics to identify enrollment retention strategies and streamline eligibility processes.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG compliance scrutiny
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG oversight creates heightened compliance requirements and potential audit exposure for large state programs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated OIG compliance monitoring team to proactively address work plan items before formal review.

riskOpportunity.label: Interoperability standards mandate
riskOpportunity.description: New federal interoperability requirements create technology modernization opportunities across state Medicaid systems.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position interoperability consulting services as states prepare for federal compliance deadlines.

riskOpportunity.label: Cost efficiency pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's $2,950 per-enrollee cost above national average creates budget pressure requiring operational optimization.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Deploy cost reduction analytics to identify high-impact areas for administrative and clinical efficiency gains.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive displacement risk
riskOpportunity.description: Multi-state procurement activity indicates increased competition for large Medicaid consulting contracts across regions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen differentiation through proprietary IP and exclusive partnership arrangements with key technology vendors.

riskOpportunity.label: Data collection expansion
riskOpportunity.description: CMS generic information collection activities signal new reporting requirements creating system enhancement needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop standardized data collection modules that can be rapidly deployed across multiple state implementations.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 4,083,939 Medicaid enrollees as of December 2025.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment decreased by 2.2% year-over-year.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,950 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks 30th of 50 states with a moderate quality score of 62 out of 100.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active procurement portals are tracking Texas HHSC opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Five OIG and regulatory items are currently active in oversight and regulatory processes.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone dates are provided in the current dataset.
